name: Castro Laboreiro Plateau Megalithic Loop
name_original: Giro ad anello da Lamas de Mouro verso Castro Laboreiro — Parque Nacional da Peneda-Gerês
description: This difficult 30.7-mile mountain biking loop in Peneda-Gerês National Park explores ancient megalithic sites and diverse terrain.
region_intro: Mountain biking around Peneda-Gerês National Park, Portugal's only national park, features diverse and rugged landscapes, including towering mountains, granite cliffs, and deep river valleys. The park encompasses the Serra da Peneda and Serra do Gerês mountain ranges, offering varied terrain for cyclists. Ancient forests and remnants of the Roman Geira road also characterize the region, providing a mix of natural and historical elements for mountain bike trails. This area provides a network of trails suitable for various skill levels. Best mountain bike trails around Peneda-Gerês National Park The most popular mountain bike trail is Castro Laboreiro Plateau Megalithic Loop, a 30.7 miles (49.4 km) trail that takes 4 hours 29 minutes to complete. This challenging route traverses ancient megalithic sites and offers significant elevation gain. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Geira and Arrieiros Trail Loop, a difficult 27.2 miles (43.7 km) path. This route follows sections of the ancient Roman Geira, offering historical context amidst rugged landscapes. Local mountain bikers also love the Lamas de Mouro and Castro Laboreiro Loop, a 21.1 miles (34.0 km) trail leading through river valleys and traditional villages, often completed in about 2 hours 55 minutes. Mountain biking around Peneda-Gerês National Park is defined by granite cliffs, ancient forests, and river views. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging routes with significant elevation. The routes in Peneda-Gerês National Park are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 20 reviews. More than 400 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Peneda-Gerês National Park's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when mountain biking in Peneda-Gerês National Park?
A: Peneda-Gerês National Park offers diverse and rugged landscapes, including towering mountains, granite cliffs, and deep river valleys. You'll find trails traversing ancient forests, sections of the Roman Geira, and traditional villages. Some routes feature significant elevation gains and can be quite demanding, occasionally requiring riders to push their bikes, while others offer more moderate experiences.

Q: Are there mountain bike trails suitable for beginners in Peneda-Gerês National Park?
A: Yes, while many trails are moderate to difficult, there is at least one easy mountain bike route available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2328114773/castro-laboreiro-plateau-megalithic-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Laboreiro Plateau Megalithic Trail&lt;/a&gt; is rated as moderate, but offers a less strenuous option compared to some of the more challenging loops.

Q: When is the best time of year to go mountain biking in Peneda-Gerês National Park?
A: The best seasons for mountain biking in Peneda-Gerês National Park are spring and autumn. During these times, the weather is generally pleasant, and trail conditions are optimal, making for a more enjoyable experience. Summers can be hot, and winters may bring challenging conditions.

Q: Are there any waterfalls or natural pools accessible from mountain bike trails?
A: Yes, Peneda-Gerês National Park is famous for its numerous waterfalls and crystal-clear natural pools. Notable attractions include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3728161" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Fecha de Barjas Waterfall (Tahiti)&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4280563" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pincães Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;. Many of these are accessible and offer beautiful spots to connect with nature.

Q: What historical or cultural sites can I see while mountain biking in the park?
A: The park integrates rich human history and culture. You can explore traditional granite villages, medieval monasteries, and sections of the ancient Roman Geira, a military road. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2328168170/castro-laboreiro-plateau-megalithic-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Laboreiro Plateau Megalithic Loop&lt;/a&gt; traverse ancient megalithic sites, offering a glimpse into the region's past.

Q: Can I find mountain bike routes with panoramic views in Peneda-Gerês National Park?
A: Absolutely. The park's dramatic landscapes, defined by the Serra da Peneda and Serra do Gerês mountain ranges, offer numerous viewpoints. Locations like Pedra Bela provide breathtaking panoramic vistas of the valleys, lagoons, and surrounding mountains, making for spectacular scenery during your ride.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes available?
A: Yes, many of the mountain bike trails in Peneda-Gerês National Park are designed as loops. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2328168170/castro-laboreiro-plateau-megalithic-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castro Laboreiro Plateau Megalithic Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2328130265/lamas-de-mouro-and-castro-laboreiro-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lamas de Mouro and Castro Laboreiro Loop&lt;/a&gt;, allowing you to start and finish at the same point.

Q: What do other mountain bikers enjoy most about mountain biking in Peneda-Gerês National Park?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 20 reviews. Mountain bikers often praise the diverse terrain, the mix of natural beauty and historical elements, and the challenging yet rewarding routes that traverse granite cliffs, ancient forests, and offer stunning river views.

Q: Are there any specific permits required for mountain biking in Peneda-Gerês National Park?
A: Peneda-Gerês is Portugal's only national park, and while general access is permitted, it's always advisable to check the official park regulations for any specific permits required for certain activities or areas, especially for larger groups or organized events. You can find more information on the official tourism website: &lt;a href="https://www.visitportoandnorth.travel/en/what-to-do/peneda-geres-national-park" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;visitportoandnorth.travel&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What are some challenging mountain bike routes for experienced riders?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, the park offers several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2328500717/geira-and-arrieiros-trail-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Geira and Arrieiros Trail Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 43.7 km path with over 1,100m of elevation gain, following sections of the ancient Roman Geira. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2328177652/ring-route-from-lamas-de-mouro-towards-mosteiro-de-fiaes" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ring route from Lamas de Mouro towards Mosteiro de Fiães&lt;/a&gt;, which covers over 65 km with significant elevation.

Q: Are there options for electric bikes (e-bikes) on the trails?
A: Yes, options for both traditional and electric bikes are available. E-bikes can be particularly helpful for exploring the park's challenging terrain and significant elevation gains, allowing more visitors to experience the diverse landscapes.

Q: What are the typical distances and elevation gains for mountain bike trails in the park?
A: Trail distances vary widely, from around 29 km for moderate routes to over 65 km for difficult ones. Elevation gains can range from approximately 500 meters for moderate trails to over 1,800 meters for the most challenging loops, reflecting the park's rugged mountainous character.
tips: Castle ruins in a dominant location on a hill, in difficult to access terrain.
The trail here is rocky and at times not easy to cycle, but you're immersed in greenery amidst the sounds, colors, and scents of nature.
The place is beautiful, nature in its splendor

faqs.question: Is this mountain biking route suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, the Castro Laboreiro Plateau Megalithic Loop is classified as a difficult mountain biking route. It requires very good fitness and advanced riding skills due to its length (approximately 49.4 km), significant elevation gain (around 930 meters), and challenging terrain that includes rocky sections and singletrack.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this mountain biking route?
faqs.answer: The terrain on the Castro Laboreiro Plateau is varied. You'll encounter hard-packed dirt and rocky sections, which can be technically challenging for mountain biking. The route also includes a mix of paved roads, paths, and significant singletrack sections, traversing low-lying vegetation, pastureland, and some oak woods.

faqs.question: What historical or natural landmarks will I encounter on the trail?
faqs.answer: This route is rich in historical and natural sights. You'll cycle through one of the largest megalithic necropolises on the Iberian Peninsula, with numerous prehistoric monuments like burial mounds and dolmens. Key highlights include the Castro Laboreiro Castle, a Rocky Forest Trail in Peneda-Gerês National Park, and the Lamas de Mouro Gateway – Peneda-Gerês National Park. You may also see 'brandas' – traditional seasonal settlements.

faqs.question: Where can I park and access the trailhead for this mountain bike route?
faqs.answer: The loop typically starts from Lamas de Mouro, which serves as a gateway to the Peneda-Gerês National Park. You can find parking facilities there, such as near the Lamas de Mouro Gateway, providing convenient access to the start of the route.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this mountain biking loop?
faqs.answer: For experienced mountain bikers, this 49.4 km loop with approximately 930 meters of elevation gain typically takes around 4 hours and 29 minutes to complete. This estimate assumes a good level of fitness and advanced riding skills.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other trails in the area?
faqs.answer: Yes, this mountain biking route intersects with or runs concurrently with several other official trails. You'll find sections that are part of the Megalitismo (41%), Planalto de Castro Laboreiro (28%), Caminho da Geira e dos Arrieiros (18%), and Trilho Castrejo (14%) trails, among others.

faqs.question: Is the Castro Laboreiro Plateau Megalithic Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are generally allowed in Peneda-Gerês National Park, it's crucial to keep them on a leash, especially in areas with livestock or wildlife, and to clean up after them. Given the challenging nature of this mountain biking route, consider if it's suitable for your dog's fitness and paw safety on rocky terrain.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to mountain bike this route?
faqs.answer: The spring and autumn months are generally the best times to mountain bike this route. The weather is typically milder, and the landscape is vibrant. Summer can be very hot, and winter may bring colder temperatures, rain, or even snow at higher elevations, making conditions more challenging.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required to access the Peneda-Gerês National Park or this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific entrance fees or permits required to access the Peneda-Gerês National Park or to ride this particular mountain biking trail. However, always respect park regulations, especially concerning waste disposal, fire prevention, and wildlife protection.

faqs.question: Are there facilities like cafes or restaurants near the trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, the nearby village of Castro Laboreiro offers amenities such as cafes and restaurants where you can refuel before or after your ride. Lamas de Mouro, a common starting point, also has some basic facilities.

faqs.question: Are there any specific sections where I might need to push my bike?
faqs.answer: Given the 'difficult' classification and the presence of rocky and challenging singletrack sections, riders should be prepared for parts of the route where pushing your bike may be necessary, especially if you encounter particularly technical ascents or descents.
